metadata.title: Differences in Circulating Gluconeogenic Substrates During Short-Term Fasting in Men, Women, and Children
metadata.study_design: Comparative fasting study with three groups: 20 adults (10 men and 10 women) fasted for 86 hr and 15 children fasted for 30 hr
metadata.demographics: Men (n=10): age 24.0±1.9 yr, height 181±2 cm, weight 73.7±2.7 kg; Women (n=10): age 23.7±1.1 yr, height 168.0±1.3 cm, weight 58.3±1.7 kg; Children (n=15, boys/girls=6/9): age 6.1±0.8 yr, height 114.2±5.9 cm, weight 21.5±2.5 kg

evidence.quote: To determine whether the differences in fasting glucose responses of men, women and children could be related to differences in glucoregulatory hormone secretion or availability of circulating gluconeogenic substrates, 20 adults (10 men and 10 women) were fasted for 86 hr and 15 children (6.1 _+ 0.8 yr, mean + SE) were fasted for 30 hr.

Table 1. Demographic Data of the Subjects Studied Age (yr) Height (cm) Weight (kg) Men (n = 10) 24.0 _+ 1.9 181 ± 2 73.7 ± 2.7 Women (n = 10) 23.7 ± 1.1 168.0 _+ 1.3 58.3 ± 1.7 Children (boys/girls = 6/9) 6.1 _+ 0.8 114.2 ± 5.9 21.5 ± 2.5
20 adults (10 men and 10 women) were fasted for 86 hr
all had fasting glucose concentrations < 110 mg/dl

methods_participants.label: Inclusion Criteria - Hypoglycemia History
methods_participants.value: Children selected from patients with symptoms compatible with hypoglycemia, none previously had documented hypoglycemia, nor developed symptoms during evaluation

evidence.quote: The 15 children were selected from patients who had had symptoms which were compatible with hypoglycemia and were fasted as part of their clinical evaluation. None previously had documented hypoglycemia, nor developed symptoms or signs of hypoglycemia during their evaluation

methods_participants.label: Fasting Protocol - Timing
methods_participants.value: Following an evening meal (1700-1800 hr), bloods obtained between 1800-2000 hr, 0800 hr, and subsequently at 4 hr intervals

evidence.quote: All individuals were fasted following an evening meal (1700 1800 hr). Bloods were obtained between 1800-2000 hr, 0800 hr, and subsequently at 4 hr intervals.

methods_participants.label: Assay - Glucose, Alanine, Glutamine, Lactate, Pyruvate, Ketone Bodies

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose, alanine, glutamine and glutamate and blood lactate, pyruvate, beta-bydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate were determined microfluorometrically

methods_participants.label: Amino Acid Analysis Method
methods_participants.value: Spinco-Beckman 119 Amino Acid Analyzer using standard physiologic methodology

evidence.quote: Amino acid determinations were performed on Spinco-Beckman 119 Amino Acid Analyzer using standard physiologic methodol- ogy
cortisol by a protein binding assay

methods_participants.label: Amino Acid Sampling - Men and Women

evidence.quote: and on the 0, 14, 22, and 30 hr fasted samples of five children
Four to five milliliter of blood were

methods_participants.label: Inclusion Criteria - Diet
methods_participants.value: weight-maintaining diet prior to study; adults consuming at least 200 gm carbohydrate per day for three days prior to study

evidence.quote: were consuming a weight-maintaining diet prior to study. The adults were consuming at least 200 gm carbohydrate per day for three days prior to the study.

narrative_results.statement: Blood lactate concentrations were higher in children than in adults between 6 and 30 hr of fasting

evidence.quote: Between 6 and 30 hr of fasting, blood lactate concentrations were higher in children than in adults (p < 0.002).

narrative_results.statement: An inverse relationship was observed between plasma glucose and beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations in each group

evidence.quote: An inverse relationship (p < 0.001) was observed between plasma glucose and beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations in each group (r = 0.934, 0.925, 0.961, men, women and children, respectively).

narrative_results.statement: Beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations were higher in women than in men during the entire period of study

evidence.quote: During the entire period of study, beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations were higher in women than in men (p < 0.001)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate concentrations rose more rapidly and to higher values in the children (3.75 _+ 0.35 mM and 1.26 _+ 0.12 mM, respectively) than in the adults (0.91 _ 0.15 mM and 0.38 +_ 0.06 mM, respectively in the men and 1.68 _+ 0.20 mM and 0.62 _+ 0.06 mM in the women) over the initial 30 hr fasting (p < 0.001).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate concentrations rose more rapidly and to higher values in the children (3.75 _+ 0.35 mM and 1.26 _+ 0.12 mM, respectively) than in the adults

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: During the entire period of study, beta-hydroxybutyrate concentrations were higher in women than in men (p < 0.001)

semantic_groups.rationale: Both report 15 children; pass_2 adds age information (6.1 ± 0.8 yr) not in pass_1, but core fact is identical
Identical facts with only capitalization differences in labels
Identical facts with only capitalization differences in labels
Both state fasting glucose < 110 mg/dl; pass_1 includes 'concentrations' descriptor but same criterion
Same inclusion criteria; pass_1 explicitly states 'consuming a weight-maintaining diet' while pass_2 omits 'consuming' but meaning is identical
pass_1 mentions only hypoglycemia history; pass_2 adds 'no family history of diabetes' which is additional criterion not mentioned in pass_1
pass_1 contains information about children selection criteria; pass_2 is empty/missing this data
Both describe same blood sampling timing; pass_2 adds context about evening meal timing (1700-1800 hr) before the 1800-2000 hr sampling, providing more complete protocol description
Both describe 4-5 ml blood collection volume; minor formatting differences (hyphen vs 'to', 'milliliter' vs 'milliliters')
Both confirm microfluorometric method for glucose assay; pass_2 provides broader context listing multiple analytes but same method applies
Both identify Spinco-Beckman 119 Amino Acid Analyzer with standard physiologic methodology; capitalization difference only
Both specify radioimmunoassay for glucagon; minor plural vs singular form difference
Both specify radioimmunoassay for insulin; minor plural vs singular form difference
Both specify radioimmunoassay for growth hormone; minor plural vs singular form difference
Both specify protein binding assay for cortisol; identical method descriptions
Both describe amino acid sampling in six men and six women at all sampling times; capitalization difference only
Both describe the same amino acid sampling procedure on fasted samples at identical time points (0, 14, 22, 30 hr) from five children. Only capitalization differs ('sampling' vs 'Sampling').

Both describe the same participant group (20 adults, 10 men, 10 women). The difference is in labeling: 'Participant N' vs 'Study Population', which are semantically equivalent descriptors for the same information.
Identical text across both extractions
Identical text across both extractions despite lower alignment score
